Jesus Christ. I am still riding a '96 Specialized M2 Comp Stump Jumper. That bike has taken me everywhere in all conditions. New young riders over complicate the living shit out of riding, and in my opinion have ruined it. I've been on rides recently with these younger gravel nerds and they have bikes covered in gadgets and bull shit. Strip it down, ride light and fast, enjoy yourself and  fuck the data. I'm a data analyst by trade and when I ride, the last thing I want is to worry about numbers  and stare a little fucking screen. I rode ALL over Colorado before GPS. Read a map and learn how to navigate. If you need a video like this to figure out bikes, you're not riding enough.

I’m not surprised by the results! My Pinarello gravel bike keeps up effortlessly with the group on road rides. It's super quick and agile. While it’s not quite the same ride as my Bianchi road bike, it’s fun, fast, and comfortable. I love taking my Pinarello on unknown roads. You never know when the pavement might turn to gravel or dirt!

Comparing two of my gravel bikes, I do notice a speed difference with my Basso on the road, but switching tire brands, it really helped bring that up. Plus, the Basso has a different setup as a 1x13, making it a distinct bike altogether. While it may not match the Pinarello’s speed, I really enjoy how my Basso performs overall on any surface!

Thanks for the awesome video. It addressed my thought on this topic. Now let’s get out on our bikes and enjoy the weather while we can!

I'm leaning towards gravel because I am looking for comfort and versatility in a single bike.  I understand the differences between these two for an experienced roadie, but how about endurance geo versus more race-y geo?  Si is likely more flexible than the majority of us, so being in a more aero position longer probably doesn't detract from his experience on a given ride -- whereas for many of us normal folk, it may.

I am today what people call a gravel biker. I started out calling myself a mountain biker but realized that I just like to cover ground, whether on road or trail. I did 15 miles this morning, mostly on country roads with lots of climbs. I have 1995 Cannondale Cad3 frame build. I love it. I love having lockable from suspension (Headshock). It’s there when I want it and gone when I don’t. I run a 2” tire with center bead (for lower role resistance) with aggressive sides for trail grip. I run them at 50 pounds. I don’t get why “gravel bikers resist mild front suspension. The 90s headshock is mild by today’s standard for mountain bikes but perfect for taking the edge off and improving control on mild single track, two track and gravel  I can’t imagine riding without it. I like my wrists. I’m 60 and still a pretty fast rider. My bike is what I consider today an expedition machine. I take journeys, not rides. I like my bike to be able to excell in to what ever the journey delivers. That means, my bike is a compromise, and that is ok. I bought that frame in 1995 and will stick with it. It fits me. You just upgrade parts when you find something that makes sense.
Nice interview. What brands will be the first to have 32inch gravel?

Yes, it's noticeable and can be important. I'm no racer and my gravel bike geometry is closer to a road geometry than some mega-adventure gravel bikes, BUT they do feel different - as you say, particularly out of the saddle.
However the 2 wheelsets is a great idea if you're going away for a cycling holiday and want to do road and gravel rides.
You don't address gearing; 1x gearing would surely be a real disadvantage when riding a gravel bike as a road bike - you'd either spin out, or have big enough gaps in your gears to make an unpleasant difference.

Hmm I like riding my Gravel Bike. I prefer to call it all road because it’s a mixture at least here in my area of gravel, forest trails but also a lot of roads in between. I wonder why everyone is seeking to use big to huge tires. I love riding on 35 or 38mm as this is a good compromise when riding roads. Am I wrong with it? Shall I switch to 45 or even 50mm only for the comfort during gravel or trails? When I calculate how much off-road I have in comparison to road it’s about 50 percent - sometimes less. But riding a roadbike wouldn’t be an option as there are too many tracks which require a gravel bike.
It seems like nobody talks about this situation. Am I alone with it? Am I too dumb to plan my tours either for road cycling or gravel only?
Over the years I noticed how important wheels are. As a heavier rider, I hope to get a good gravel soon and help myself with the maximum system weight limit by investing in a good wheel set

And since I ride mostly gravel and make it a point in keeping up with the roadies, I am actually chuffed that I'm 10% better than them. :D

Cool idea - the only thing I'd do differently is to put on some old jeans, hug the bag, cut one corner and just walk around and leak it onto the gravel until all my bags are empty. Or cut a bag corner then drag it until empty. This would help distribute it more evenly then you can rake it in. I'd use the back side of the broom or rake to road grade it level before pouring water.

@brianridesusa5126  True enough, but the same bike can feel very different with minor adjustments. I've got a cyclocross, and have experimented with e.g. moving the spacers from under the stem to slamming it, flipping the stem upside-down, shimmying the seat up and down or back and forth etc. a few millimetres, and those make a huge difference. Hell, replacing the brake cable casings into non-compressible ones unexpectedly made me ride faster because I have better brake feel resulting in more confidence in the bike.

Having said that, I reckon a typical gravel bike would generally feel easier and less intimidating to ride out of the box, and is therefore better suited for a novice.

i'm all for gravel getting more people on bikes. There shouldn't be just a road and mountain bike option. That said, i love my 2010 cyclocross bike that hits all the dirt I care to, and it has plenty of tire clearance.

@aliensporebomb  i did a 340km weekend on my gravel bike with 34mm slicks. Was good. Hills easy because of lower gearing. But i think my road bike may have been fsster. Justas comfortable really with 28mm tires. I need a better seat for thegravel bike. Mine iz a touch too wide and gives alot of chaffing after 140km

Feel agility emotion, i.e. all of them tangibles are inextricably linked to experience. A bike that is more on the agile side will be difficult for an inexperienced rider on the descents, as well as on the steep climbs. A bike that is less agile, a gravel bike, maybe found to be better suited to an inexperienced road rider.

The prototype Scott 32er Gravel bike ridden at Unbound looked very put together.   Once the big brands start producing 32ers, I think 32ers will catch on.   I think we're maybe a year away from 32ers turning the bike industry upside down just like 29ers did.  Build me a full-sus 32er Epic or a 32er Crux that fits my 5'9" height and I'll buy one.

Well I bought a gravel bike frame (Titanium, but I want this bike frame to last me forever, carbon wears out over time) before watching this video, but it checks all of the boxes (50mm tire clearance, 27.2 round seat post & standard steam for handlebars) except the frame will not have any internal frame storage, which I do not believe will be a huge deal for me.  Well the components that I bought are mechanical disc brakes as well, but I heard someone say I can change out the brake pads to more grippy and closer to hydraulic disc brakes so I'm going to try that.  That said, I'm not intending to go down any super technical hills/single track, so I don't think it'll be an issue, but nothing that can't be changed over time either.
inb4 gravel bikes evolve into mtbs for the 2nd time

Biggest problem I see is guys trying to use a Gravel bike in MTB terrain. To me, a Gravel is an awesome road bike thats comfortable and can do some dirt roads as well. If you’re mainly riding off-road and single-track, use an MTB and you’ll be far happier and safer.

To me its all about aesthetics and feel. I grew up riding commuter bikes, and only relatively recently rode a modern road bike. Now I'm completely addicted to road bikes. I am totally with Si, even if we know the gravel bike is more practical and only slightly slower.
